A couple of weeks later the tenant in the mother-in-law cottage at the back of our property knocked on the door and asked if I needed a jacket. He had been visiting a friend who was confined to a nursing home. The friend had found a strange jacket in the closet of his room and passed it on to our tenant. It was indubitably my lost jacket. Mass produced and a common style and color; but I had mended the lining in several places by hand and it was absolutely my lost jacket. How it got from a restaurant on one side of town to the closet of a stranger who never left his nursing home on the other side of town, and who just happened to be friends with our tenant is a mystery. replies.title: bad habits
replies.content:
Years ago my great uncle described his self-invented method for quitting smoking. He said that he never told himself that he was quitting. He was just cutting back. He continued to carry a pack in his front shirt pocket. Each time he thought of lighting up he would say to himself "not right now, maybe later." He said sometimes he would have a cigarette all the way to his lips, ready to light before pulling back and returning it to the pack. He didn't say whether he ever lit up and took a few puffs before regaining control. Eventually the pack he had been carrying became so old and stale that he thew them out and never bought more.

My mother's family seems to have a way of developing weird coping mechanisms. When she was dealing with what, from her descriptions, was obviously serious post-partem depression my grandmother spent several months plucking her leg hairs until they became discouraged and gave up. My mother also managed to quit drinking on her own, without any medical aid or 12-step program. It took about 3 tries, but has stuck and that was after about 10 years of seriously out of control consumption. If I asked her how I'm sure she would echo her mother's statement that "you just have to make up your mind."

Many years ago I read a book on the subject of wight loss. It contained the tale of an overweight woman who was visiting relatives in a small Norwegian town. Being fat she was used to the assumption that her obesity was caused by lack of will power. She was praising pizza to them and was astonished to learn that they had never eaten pizza. The next day she went to town--no pizza to be had. Undeterred she caught the train to the capital; no pizza there either. She went to specialty food shops, but no one carried Italian cheese or sausage. Finally she went to the airport, flew to Rome, purchased the ingredients and returned to her relatives to bake and serve a proper pizza. The point of the story was that she obviously did not lack willpower. Nothing was going to stand in the say of showing her kin what a delightful food pizza was. So lack of willpower was not the obstacle to losing weight, some other factor was, some psychological factor that she had to master before she could turn her will power to a healthier relation to food.

Rita

replies.title: Re: bad habits

My Dad quit smoking in a nearly identical way: he hasn't smoked in decades, but maintains to this day that he didn't actually quit, he could have a smoke at any time. He's just not going to do it right now. Maybe later. He kept a pack in his car glove box for many years, to maintain this narrative. Never opened it.

I have just finished working through the final chapter of Learning Ritual Magic. Just wanted to say thanks to you and your co-authors for a wonderful book. I have noticed several real and tangible changes to my life. These are not big, earth-shattering philosophical revelations but everyday matters.

I am now more calm and focused than I was previously. I notice this in little things. For example, I speak clearer and with more purpose. I don't say "umm". I don't struggle to explain myself. When typing on the computer I make fewer mistakes than I used to. When moving I seem to move with more fluidity somehow. Another benefit is that I pay more attention to the world. These are not things I was trying to improve on and I had no expectation that they would improve from doing the course but it seems they have. Perhaps the fact that I even notice these things about myself is also part of the improvements (the background awareness work).

As I reflect on the course, I'm reminded that my primary motivation for starting the work was curiosity. It seemed like the concept of magic was popping up in several unrelated places and I wanted to find out what it was about. As I think of my next steps, I'm also wondering about motivations.

I recall that at the start of the book you mention that the correct answer is "I want to learn in order to serve". I am still not sure what is meant by that. Perhaps this due to the solitary nature of the work involved in Learning Ritual Magic and also the fact that work is personal in nature. The best interpretation I can think of is "as above, so below": by working on oneself, one also improves the whole. Or is it that this phase of working on myself is a preparation for the work which comes later that serves higher purposes?

I'd be grateful for any guidance you can give on this.

Simon

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Daily practice of the SoP is a central part of the Dolmen Arch course.

2) We don't have any ancient texts relating to Druidry -- just a handful of accounts by Greek and Roman tourists and a somewhat larger handful of stories written down by Christian monks centuries after the last Druid died. The three Druidical elements are from the writings of Iolo Morganwg.

3) The word karma in Sanskrit means "action," and the rule of karma is simply that actions have consequences that affect the actor. The kind of naive understanding you've described misses the whole point. Your actions have consequences, in this and future lives -- and yes, meditation and divination are good ways to help you set in motion the consequences you prefer.

4) We don't know what the ancient Celts did or didn't teach on the subject. In modern Druid lore, we differentiate between fate, which is the sum of the consequences of previous actions (basically, karma); destiny, which is the direction your higher self is trying to lead you; and will, which is what you choose to do right here and now. Each of these is as strong as the others, and so any two of them can outweigh the third; a lot of the point of magical training is learning to sense your destiny so that you can align your will with it and overcome your fate.

5) Of course there are points of contact; that's why an act of will can move your hand. Yes, they can be created artificially; that's what a talisman is, for example.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's the normal condition of most people, and explains why most people have aimless lives. You can change that by aligning your will with your destiny -- or, less pleasantly, your will with your fate.

Or let's say your destiny is pulling you toward an artistic career but your fate pulls you toward abandoning your creativity in order to try to get rich, and you decide on the latter and commit your ego to the choice. Again, it happens all the time.

replies.title: Re: people going missing in the wilderness
replies.content:
Far more often that not, a missing person has simply gotten lost in the woods and died there from starvation and exhaustion. Human skeletons turn up there from time to time, according to people who spend a lot of time in the woods. It is extremely easy to get lost in any unfamiliar landscape that is generally empty of people.

www.ecosophia.net/blogs-and-essays/the-well-of-galabes/how-not-to-learn-magic-an-introductory-note/ )

These seem to me to be two different phenomena. The "eating places" kill you in This World, and your remains may be found and recovered. The "doorways" take you into one of the Other Worlds, from which you may never return.

replies.title: Re: Geomantic ritual magic

Jean-Pierre, if I may...

Remember that Rubeus in the 1st house is one of the classic warning signs, normally suggesting that the querent has a hidden motive, or is not being fully honest with the geomancer. It's especially problematic when Rubeus passes from the 1st to the house of the question... which in this case is quite plausibly the 8th (having to do with spiritual entities and magical practice), though in the post that you wrote here, the exact phrasing of the question was not entirely clear.

I would recommend pausing, taking a deep breath, and then reflecting on what your deeper motivations are here. Since you are both the querent and the geomancer, is there some self-deception that you need to notice and acknowledge? The message of Rubeus *may* go deeper here, if the self-deception is in regard to the message about which you're asking the question. That is, if the problem is not about the divination itself, then Rubeus in 1st and 8th would be a sign that you're deluding yourself or confused in some way about the apparent message received in the prayer.

In general, Carcer tends to indicate people or things pulling in opposite directions: one party is trying to go one way, while the other party is trying to do just the opposite. It indicates a failure to come together, a failure to connect. Remember also that the inverse figure of Carcer is Conjunctio. Conjunctio indicates things coming together effectively, and as a figure of Mercury, Conjunctio especially speaks to effective communication between two parties. The fact that the Judge was *not* Conjuncio, but its polar opposite, Carcer, indicates a failure of effective communication. Puer as the Sentence/Reconciler would then indicate rashly but foolishly charging ahead anyway.

Some Hindu writings (e.g., the Srimad Bhagavatam suggest that the various divine names -- Brahma, Vishnu, etc. -- are offices rather than individuals, and different souls fill those positions in different ages. Some Western occult writings suggest that being a god or goddess of the sort that interacts with human beings on Earth is simply one more stage on the upward path, and the gods of antiquity have passed beyond that stage and been replaced by other beings. These two views are by no means incompatible, of course.

In Shinto lore, there are said to be eight hundred myriads of kami -- that is, eight million gods. Hindu sources suggest that this is a considerable understatement. comments.title: UFO’s
comments.content:
JMG,

I’m with you on your assessment of the UFO phenomenon, i.,e., they are military sleight of hand to cover up testing of new military aircraft, along with the occasional metaphysical manifestation accounting for some of them. But here’s something that has me wondering - whereas in the past the military has used the UFO cover for high-altitude balloon surveillance and Stealth aircraft, the recent UFO military announcement was accompanied by a video clip taken from a fighter.jet, several of which seemed to be pursuing a truly odd object. Now, a high-altitude balloon might be mistaken for a silvery UFO and a delta-shaped Stealth aircraft could be mistaken for an alien-looking UFO, but they are still recognizable as a balloon and an aircraft respectively.

The object in the jet fighter video really does seem to be a quantum leap in military imagination, if the video is some kind of cover for a new aircraft. We can see the smallish, wingless, gravity-defying object darting about at impossibly high-speeds while we can hear the pilots exclaiming at the object’s maneuverings. The video, btw, does come across as genuine in every way, but who knows, I suppose.

Would you think the military, by taking the unusual step of releasing the video to the public, really has developed an aircraft along these pretty spectacular lines, and that it expects the public to be seeing them and thinking, whoa, there goes an alien vessel because what else could it be?

And last week, it was disclosed that the Navy came up with guidelines for the reporting of UFO’s so that pilots and Navy personnel, who are said to be seeing a lot of UFO’s recently, can report them without fear of being stigmatized as headcases. I’m thinking that if all this is military cover, the aircraft they are covering up might be truly different, and that they expect a lot of people to see them.

Or might something really unusual be going on?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: UFO’s
replies.content:
Anything -- anything -- that comes from military sources can be assumed to be part of the disinformation game. Ask yourself this: does the Pentagon have the funding to pay for a film in which a CGI image of a UFO does all kinds of things, while actors playing pilots respond accordingly? Of course they do. The thing in the video won't be the new technology they're testing, but it'll look just enough like the new technology that they will be confused -- just as the "black triangles" they deployed over various populated areas in the early 1980s looked just enough like stealth planes (the secret aerospace project then being hidden under cover of the UFO business) that they kept everyone guessing.

You can be good and sure, by the way, that anything that actually gets reported to the public by Navy personnel will have been cleared, and may well have been invented, by Naval Intelligence. The US has a long track record with this kind of thing, going back to those inflatable tanks they used to fake out the Germans in advance of D-Day, and they're good at it.

comments.title: Concealed shoes
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

A friend of mine told me a story about discovering a shoe that had been intentionally concealed in the walls of an 1800s-era house in Northwestern Connecticut. The shoe was discovered by her grandfather, who seemed to think this was a bit of folk magic brought over by English settlers, and that the location of the shoe near the chimney was significant.

It reminded me a bit of Witch Bottles, which are also concealed in the walls of homes. When I looked into a bit of research I found conflicting theories for the purpose of the shoes. Some sources claim the shoes are there to ward off or to entrap Witches or other hostile entities. Other sources claim the purpose was to attract beneficial house spirits, such as brownies.

Do you have a particular view on this practice and the purpose behind it?

Many thanks!

replies.title: Re: Concealed shoes
replies.content: Hmm! No, I don't think I've ever heard of that custom. Anybody else?

replies.title: Re: Concealed shoes
replies.content: Hidden shoes are found pretty often during house renovations.  It was just an easy thing one did (in the English-speaking world, at least) up until the early 1800s, though less often as the centuries rolled on.  So far as I know, there's no surviving old lore about what it's supposed to accomplish and just how it manages to accomplish it.

replies.title: Re: Concealed shoes
replies.content:
It was apparently pretty common until the 20th century, but the exact reasons for it are not at all clear. I've heard everything from "fertility charm" to "substitute for human sacrifice". (shrugs)

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Concealed_shoes

replies.title: Re: Concealed shoes
replies.content:
My grandfather once found a very old shoe in the ceiling of his farmhouse in southern Maine. My father brought it to an antiques guy who'd heard it was common during a certain period to conceal a shoe inside the structure of a house. He estimated that this particular shoe was probably made in the late 18th/early 19th century.
A historian in the UK has cataloged finds like these, more than a thousand, in the Concealed Shoe Index at the Northampton Museum. They're concentrated in England and New England. If I recall the details correctly, it's almost always a single shoe, not a pair, usually well worn, and very often in a child's size. A search for "concealed shoes" will get you a lot of articles with speculations about the origins and purposes of the practice.

comments.title: Sacred places
comments.content:
Certain places have been considered sacred for a long time. The evidence for deposits and offerings often goes back to the Neolithic we can see layers of building, from simple shrines or cairns, altars, and then big temples were built up over time. Even in the case of another group of people coming in, they usually continued to revere the older sacred sites(think of Bronze Age European reverence for Neolithic burial sites). I suspect that the Catholic Church understood sacred places well, as they built their major churches on or near much older sacred sites. They even made sure to paper over the local cults with newly minted saints.

Why have people gathered at such places for offerings and eventually temples and shrines for so long? What made such places recognized as sacred? Have modern people lost the sense that allowed the ancients to detect such places?

replies.title: Re: Sacred places
replies.content:
In Druid teaching, there are two primary currents of magical energy, the solar current and the telluric current. The solar current descends to earth from the heavens and is influenced by time -- variations in the solar current are what astrology tracks. The telluric current ascends from the center of the earth and is influenced by place -- sacred places are those places where some aspect of the telluric current flows strongly.

We all still have the subtle senses that enable us to perceive the currents. We're just taught, with as much bullying as necessary, to ignore them. (Watch the way most parents and teachers treat a child who experiences something that's been officially classified as Not Real.) One of the basic stages of magical training, in turn, is getting past that childhood programming and using the senses we've got, to perceive the things that everyone perceived as a matter of course in ancient and medieval times.
